name: Practice Bidding Scenarios
description: Bidding and play-of-the-hand lessons curated for the Practice Bidding Scenarios project.

lessons.name: What Should I Open?
lessons.description: South opens with a balanced-looking hand that is too weak for 1NT, so the choice is which suit to name first.

lessons.name: How to Respond
lessons.description: Partner opened; with about 6 or more points you answer, keeping the bidding alive and starting to describe your hand.

lessons.name: Opener's Rebid
lessons.description: You opened and partner responded; now your rebid shows your strength and shape in one more call.

lessons.name: Open the Suit That Buys You a Rebid
lessons.description: South is a minimum holding two suits; opening the longer one can leave no comfortable rebid, so sometimes you open the other suit first.

lessons.name: Minor Suit Openings
lessons.description: South opens a minor and the partnership hunts for a fit at a comfortable level.

lessons.name: Major Suit Openings
lessons.description: South opens a major and North has a strong hand of his own.

lessons.name: 1NT Openings
lessons.description: South opens a strong balanced hand and North must judge whether the partnership has enough for game.

lessons.name: 1NT Balanced Raise
lessons.description: Partner opens 1NT and you are balanced with no four- or five-card major, so there is no Stayman or transfer to make. Add your points to partner's 15-17 and raise notrump to the level it calls for: pass, 2NT to invite, 3NT for game, and quantitatively 4NT, 6NT, or 7NT with slam values. From the responder's chair.

lessons.name: Overcalls
lessons.description: East opens, and South sits next with a real suit and some values.

lessons.name: Takeout Doubles
lessons.description: East opens 1♣ and the next hand holds the classic takeout shape — short in clubs with the other three suits covered.

lessons.name: Weak Two-Bids
lessons.description: A disciplined preempt, an inquiry, and partner driving to game.

lessons.name: Fourth Suit Forcing
lessons.description: After a 1-level response and a new suit by opener, bid the fourth suit as an artificial game force, asking opener for major support, a stopper, or extra shape.

lessons.name: Preemptive Major Raise
lessons.description: Partner opens a major and you hold a weak, shapely hand with five-plus trumps: jump straight to game. A distributional raise, not an invitation — it bids to the level of the fit, denies slam, and steals the opponents' room. From the responder's chair.

lessons.name: 3-Card Limit Raise
lessons.description: Partner opens a major and you have invitational values (about 11-12) with only three-card support: too good for a simple raise, but a direct limit raise would promise four trumps. Start with a forcing 1NT, then jump to three of the major — showing the invitational raise on exactly three trumps, so opener can judge game precisely. From the responder's chair.

lessons.name: Spiral Raises — Wolpert
lessons.description: An eight-card fit is never guaranteed by a Spiral raise, so responder asks the cheapest step and lets opener confess three or four trumps.

lessons.name: Spiral Raises — Weinstein
lessons.description: When opener's Weinstein raise might rest on only three trumps, responder bids naturally to test for a sounder 4-4 fit before settling the game.

lessons.name: Reverse by Opener
lessons.description: Opener rebids a new, higher suit at the two level — a reverse showing 5-4 shape and extra values — and responder reads the strength to place the contract.

lessons.name: Drury
lessons.description: As a passed hand with a good major raise, bid 2♣ Drury instead of jumping to game; opener's rebid reveals whether the third- or fourth-seat opening is sound, so you reach game with extras and stop low opposite a minimum.

lessons.name: Inverted Minors
lessons.description: A single raise of partner's minor is FORCING (10+, denying a four-card major) and a jump raise is weak; opener shows stoppers toward 3NT, judges the minor game or slam, and reads the preemptive jump correctly.

lessons.name: 3NT Rebid by Opener
lessons.description: After opening one of a suit, opener jumps to 3NT - with a long, solid suit and outside values when partner is weak, or with extra values and the unbid suits stopped after a game-forcing two-over-one.

lessons.name: Jacoby 2NT
lessons.description: Partner's game-forcing major raise asks opener to describe; opener shows shortness, a side suit, or extra strength, and the partnership judges game or slam.

lessons.name: Splinters
lessons.description: Responder's double jump shows four-plus trump support with a singleton or void; opener counts whether the high cards are working — declining game when they are wasted opposite the shortness, driving to slam (or staying disciplined off a keycard) when they are controls.

lessons.name: Soloway Jump Shift
lessons.description: Partner's strong jump shift announces a slam-going hand with a good suit; opener clarifies and the partnership cue-bids toward the right slam.

lessons.name: Stayman
lessons.description: Over 1NT, 2♣ asks opener for a four-card major so the partnership can find a 4-4 major fit before settling in notrump.

lessons.name: Texas Transfer
lessons.description: Over 1NT, partner bids four of the minor below a six-card major to transfer straight to game — right-siding the major and leaving room for a keycard slam try.

lessons.name: Smolen
lessons.description: With 5-4 in the majors and game values, partner starts with Stayman then jumps in the four-card major to show five of the other — letting the 1NT opener declare the major fit.

lessons.name: Slam after Stayman
lessons.description: After you open 1NT and answer Stayman, partner explores slam - a quantitative 4NT to accept or decline, or Roman Key Card Blackwood when a major fit is found; cooperate and place the contract.

lessons.name: Slam after Stayman or Jacoby
lessons.description: After you open 1NT, partner explores slam via Stayman or a Jacoby transfer and raises to a quantitative 4NT; accept with a maximum and choose the strain - 6NT with no fit, six of the suit when a fit is known.

lessons.name: Simple Finesse
lessons.description: Lead toward an honor and finesse for a missing high card.

lessons.name: Ruffing Finesse
lessons.description: Run an honor through the defender — ruff it if covered, pitch if not: a finesse you cannot lose.

lessons.name: Double Finesse
lessons.description: Two honors are missing — finesse twice: about three chances in four that one hook wins.

lessons.name: To Finesse or Not?
lessons.description: Count first — decline a needless finesse; when you must take one, let the bidding tell you it wins.

lessons.name: Two-Way Finesse
lessons.description: Finesse for the queen of diamonds with the option of playing either defender for it.

lessons.name: Repeated Finesse
lessons.description: One finesse is not enough against a guarded honor — hook again, on entries.

lessons.name: Deep Finesse
lessons.description: The nine and ten are finesse cards too — and the odds rungs: eight ever, nine never.

lessons.name: Choice of Finesses
lessons.description: Two finesses are available — combine your chances to pick the right one.

lessons.name: Rabbi's Rule
lessons.description: Safety play — when the king is the only card out, drop the singleton king.

lessons.name: Finesse Exam
lessons.description: The final test — ten mixed hands, no labels: you name the tool, then play it.

categories.name: Play of the Hand: Notrump Play

lessons.name: Hold-Up at 3NT
lessons.description: Duck an early round to sever the defenders' long-suit communication, then claim 3NT.

lessons.name: Suit Promotion
lessons.description: Build extra winners by force, driving out the defenders' high cards.

lessons.name: Top Tricks — Notrump
lessons.description: Count and cash your sure tricks in a notrump contract.

categories.name: Play of the Hand: Suit Contract Play

lessons.name: Side-Suit Ruff Before Trumps
lessons.description: Take a side-suit ruff in dummy before drawing trumps in 4♠.

lessons.name: Strip-and-Endplay
lessons.description: Strip the hand and throw a defender in to concede the decisive trick in 4♠.

lessons.name: Play Your Top Tricks
lessons.description: Count and cash your sure winners to bring home a partscore.

lessons.name: Top Tricks — Suit Contract
lessons.description: Count and cash your sure tricks in a suit contract.

categories.name: Play of the Hand: Squeezes & Endplays

lessons.name: Strip & Endplay
lessons.description: Strip his safe exits, throw him in, and let him play your suit for you — the 50% finesse becomes 100%.

lessons.name: Simple Squeeze
lessons.description: One defender guards two suits: rectify the count, run your winners, and watch the discards.

lessons.name: Rectify the Count
lessons.description: The squeeze's ignition: give away the tricks you must lose EARLY — trick one if you can.

lessons.name: Show-Up Squeeze
lessons.description: Finesse while you must, count while you finesse — and cash when the honor stands alone.

lessons.name: Double Squeeze
lessons.description: Both defenders chained to their own suits — the third suit falls to nobody but you.

lessons.name: Squeeze & Endplay Exam
lessons.description: The shelf's final test — six mixed hands, no labels: endplays, squeezes, show-ups, and one perfectly timed duck.
